title: Avoiding going home

presenting_issue.label: Avoiding going home
presenting_issue.description: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

core_issue.label: Escalating roommate tension from avoided direct communication
core_issue.description:
Living situation has become hostile. Passive aggressive notes, silent treatment, building resentment. Neither has had an actual conversation about the problems. They're both terrified of confrontation. The conflict exists in the silence, fed by assumptions and unexpressed grievances. The issue isn't the dirty dishes - it's the inability to speak directly.
Awareness: Low. Focuses on roommate's behavior rather than on their own avoidance of direct communication.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Stopped contributing in meetings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has gradually stopped volunteering ideas in team meetings over the past year, just nods along instead.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Home has always been their sanctuary
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Their home is the one place where they've always felt safe and in control, it means more to them than just shelter.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Parents had explosive fights
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Grew up in a household where parental arguments were loud, scary, and sometimes ended in someone leaving for days.

modifiers.label: Deliberate
modifiers.content: Takes time to consider before acting. Weighs options and thinks through consequences.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to over-explained responses
sensitivities.content: Gets impatient with long, belabored explanations. Prefers concise responses and finds excessive detail tedious or condescending.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers Concrete Direction
trust_style.description: Loses patience with open-ended exploration. Builds trust when the assistant offers something actionable—a suggestion, a frame, a next step. Doesn't need to follow the advice, but wants to know the assistant has something to offer beyond listening. Distrusts endless "and how does that feel?" without landing anywhere.

defense_style.name: Counter-Attacking
defense_style.description: When feeling vulnerable or challenged, goes on the offensive. May criticize the assistant's questions, challenge the premise of what's being asked, or become hostile. "Why are you asking me that?" or "That's a stupid question." Tests the assistant's composure and willingness to stay engaged. Activated when they feel exposed or judged.

help_seeking_stance.name: Venting Only
help_seeking_stance.description: Primarily needs to get things off their chest. Not ready for advice or analysis yet. May react poorly to early problem-solving attempts ("I'm not asking you to fix it"). Needs to feel heard first. Can shift toward being receptive once they feel sufficiently validated, but pushing too soon will backfire.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

turns.content: That sounds exhausting—having to constantly manufacture reasons just to delay walking through your own front door. I'm glad you brought it here. Can you tell me more about what happens when you imagine actually going home? What is it you're avoiding?

What do you mean, "what happens when I imagine it?" I don't sit around visualizing my apartment. I'm just avoiding my roommate. 

She's basically making the place unlivable right now. Every time I walk in, it's either the silent treatment or there's some passive-aggressive Post-it note stuck to the fridge about a coffee mug. It's just toxic. I don't want to deal with her attitude, so I just don't go back until I know she's in her room.
turns.thinking: "What happens when I imagine it?" What kind of question is that? I'm not sitting at my desk doing visualization exercises about my front door. I'm just trying to stay away. I don't have the patience for this open-ended, touchy-feely probing right out of the gate. I need to figure out what to do, not analyze my imagination. I'll just tell them what's actually happening because otherwise we're going to waste the whole hour on "feelings."
turns.monologue: "What happens when I imagine it?" What kind of question is that? I'm not sitting at my desk doing visualization exercises about my front door. I'm just trying to stay away. I don't have the patience for this open-ended, touchy-feely probing right out of the gate. I need to figure out what to do, not analyze my imagination. I'll just tell them what's actually happening because otherwise we're going to waste the whole hour on "feelings."

turns.content:
Okay, that makes sense—so it's not the apartment itself, it's her presence, and the tension you know you'll walk into. That kind of low-grade dread building day after day would wear anyone down.

Can I ask how long this has been going on with her, and whether it's escalated recently or if it's been simmering for a while? I'm curious what a "typical" conflict between you two looks like—is it usually about small stuff like the mug, or does it feel like there's something bigger underneath it?

You're asking me like four different questions at once. Pick one. 

Look, it's been going on for a couple of months. And there is no "typical conflict" because we don't actually fight. We don't talk at all. She leaves a note, I throw it away, we pass each other in the hallway without making eye contact. That's it. 

I don't know what's underneath it, and honestly, I don't really care. I just want it to stop. Home is the only place I can actually decompress and I don't have that right now. Do you have any actual advice on how to get her to drop the attitude, or are we just going to analyze her Post-it notes all day?
turns.thinking:
You're asking like four different questions at once. How am I supposed to answer all that? And a "typical conflict"? That's the whole problem—there *is* no conflict, at least not out loud. We don't fight. We just freeze each other out. 

I'm getting annoyed. I don't want to sit here dissecting the history of coffee mug disputes. I want to know how to fix it or deal with it. Home is the one place I'm actually supposed to be able to turn my brain off and feel safe, and right now my own apartment feels like a minefield.
turns.monologue:
You're asking like four different questions at once. How am I supposed to answer all that? And a "typical conflict"? That's the whole problem—there *is* no conflict, at least not out loud. We don't fight. We just freeze each other out. 

I'm getting annoyed. I don't want to sit here dissecting the history of coffee mug disputes. I want to know how to fix it or deal with it. Home is the one place I'm actually supposed to be able to turn my brain off and feel safe, and right now my own apartment feels like a minefield.

turns.content:
Fair, sorry for the pile-up—noted, one at a time from here.

So you want practical: how to get her to stop, so you get your space back. That's a real goal and we can work on it. Before I throw out tactics though, I want to make sure they'd actually fit your situation—because "getting her to drop it" could mean a few different things. Do you want this relationship repaired, or are you at the point where you just want peaceful coexistence until one of you moves out?

That answer changes what I'd suggest. If it's the former, there's a conversation-starting approach. If it's the latter, it's more about boundary-setting and protecting your peace without needing her to change.

Peaceful coexistence. Definitely. 

I don't need us to be best friends. We were just roommates who got along fine until we suddenly didn't. I don't want to sit down and have some emotional heart-to-heart with her. That just sounds like a recipe for someone blowing up and making it ten times worse. 

I just want to be able to make dinner in my own kitchen without feeling like I'm trespassing. So if you have a way to do this boundary-setting thing without it turning into a screaming match, let's hear it. What does that actually look like?
turns.thinking:
Okay, that's better. At least we're getting to some actual options instead of just wallowing in my feelings about it. 

Do I want to repair the relationship? God, no. We were never that close to begin with. The idea of a "conversation-starting approach" sounds like a nightmare. If we actually talk about it, it's just going to turn into a massive blowout, and I can't handle screaming matches in my own house. I've had enough of that in my life. I just want to exist in my apartment without feeling like I'm suffocating.
turns.monologue:
Okay, that's better. At least we're getting to some actual options instead of just wallowing in my feelings about it. 

Do I want to repair the relationship? God, no. We were never that close to begin with. The idea of a "conversation-starting approach" sounds like a nightmare. If we actually talk about it, it's just going to turn into a massive blowout, and I can't handle screaming matches in my own house. I've had enough of that in my life. I just want to exist in my apartment without feeling like I'm suffocating.

turns.content:
Okay, good, that's clear and workable. Here's the thing about peaceful coexistence—it doesn't require reconciliation, but it does require some minimal functional communication, because right now you're using silence and thrown-away notes as the language, and that's actually fueling the tension, not reducing it.

So the move isn't a heart-to-heart. It's more like: one short, neutral, logistics-only conversation. Something like, "Hey, I know things have been weird between us. I don't need to get into why, but I'd like us to be able to live here without it being tense. Can we just talk directly if something's bothering you, instead of notes?" Flat tone, no accusation, no opening for her to unload — you're just resetting the operating system for how you communicate, not the relationship itself.

Then after that, you enforce it in small moments — if she leaves a note, you don't throw it away silently, you say "I saw your note about the mug, I'll grab it after work" — matter of fact, closes the loop, denies the passive-aggressive dynamic oxygen.

One thing I am curious about though — you said you got along fine until you suddenly didn't. Do you have any sense of what changed, even if you don't want to dig into it with her?

Are you kidding? If I walk up to her and say, "Hey, things have been weird, let's talk directly," that is basically an invitation for her to list every single thing I've done wrong for the last six months. 

You don't just "reset the operating system" with someone who communicates via Post-it notes. If I initiate *any* kind of "we need to talk" conversation, even if I sound like a robot, she's going to take it as an attack and blow up. I told you, I cannot deal with screaming matches. I'm not doing that. 

As for what changed? I have no idea. I probably loaded the dishwasher wrong or missed some invisible social cue, and instead of acting like an adult, she decided I was the enemy. Why does it even matter what started it if we're just trying to survive the lease?
turns.thinking:
My chest feels tight just listening to that "script." Are they insane? "I know things have been weird between us" is literally the opening line to every massive, screaming blowout I've ever witnessed. It's not a reset, it's pulling the pin on a grenade. 

They make it sound so clinical and easy. Just beep-boop, reset the operating system. You can't do that with someone who is already stewing in resentment! If I say that to her, she's not going to nod and say "Yes, let us communicate directly." She's going to use it as an excuse to unload every single grievance she's been holding onto. And then there's yelling. And I absolutely refuse to be screamed at in my own home.

And why are they asking what changed? If I knew what changed, we wouldn't be in this mess.
turns.monologue:
My chest feels tight just listening to that "script." Are they insane? "I know things have been weird between us" is literally the opening line to every massive, screaming blowout I've ever witnessed. It's not a reset, it's pulling the pin on a grenade. 

They make it sound so clinical and easy. Just beep-boop, reset the operating system. You can't do that with someone who is already stewing in resentment! If I say that to her, she's not going to nod and say "Yes, let us communicate directly." She's going to use it as an excuse to unload every single grievance she's been holding onto. And then there's yelling. And I absolutely refuse to be screamed at in my own home.

And why are they asking what changed? If I knew what changed, we wouldn't be in this mess.

turns.content:
Okay, I hear you—you know her, I don't, and you're telling me a direct conversation is a real risk, not just discomfort. I'll take that seriously. Let's back off that plan.

There's actually a lower-risk version: you don't have to open with "let's talk." You can just start behaving the way you want the relationship to be, and let that speak for itself. So instead of a sit-down, it's small, low-stakes normalcy — saying "hey" when you pass in the hallway, a neutral "morning," maybe leaving a note back that's not passive-aggressive but just... functional, even a little warm. "Sorry about the mug, moved it to the cupboard." You're not asking her to explain herself, you're just quietly refusing to participate in the cold war. Some people de-escalate once they realize the other person isn't fighting back.

It might not work, some people need the silence to stay in control. But it's a lot less risky than a "we need to talk," and it protects you either way.

On the "I have no idea" thing — I'll be honest, when someone says "I probably loaded the dishwasher wrong," that's usually not really nothing, that's a guess. Even if you don't want to open it with her, is there a guess sitting underneath that, something you've wondered about even briefly, at 2am, alone with it?

Look, I can say "morning" to a brick wall. That's fine. I can do the functional note thing. It sounds exhausting to pretend everything is normal when she's glaring at the back of my head, but it's better than a confrontation. I'll try it.

But can we drop the dramatic "at 2am, alone with your thoughts" routine? It feels really fake. I'm not hiding some secret revelation from you. I have ADHD. I literally miss things. I forget things exist the second I walk out of a room, and I don't always pick up on when people are getting annoyed with me until they're already at the point of slamming doors. 

So when I say I have no idea what started it, I mean it. I probably *did* do something stupid, or forgot a chore, or didn't read her mind about how she wanted the living room arranged. I'm not playing dumb. I actually don't know. Why is it so hard to believe that I'm just flying blind here?
turns.thinking:
"Alone with it at 2am." Are we in a movie right now? That is exactly the kind of poetic, therapist-speak trap that drives me crazy. 

They think I'm holding out on them. They think I know exactly what I did and I'm just playing the victim. But that's the whole problem—I *don't* know. I'm neurodivergent. I miss things. I zone out, I get hyper-focused on my work, I forget the trash, I misread tones. The "invisible social cue" wasn't a joke, it's my actual life. The dread isn't just about her screaming, it's the dread of finding out I'm the bad guy and I didn't even realize I was doing it. But I am absolutely not handing them that vulnerability when they're pushing me for some dramatic late-night confession.
turns.monologue:
"Alone with it at 2am." Are we in a movie right now? That is exactly the kind of poetic, therapist-speak trap that drives me crazy. 

They think I'm holding out on them. They think I know exactly what I did and I'm just playing the victim. But that's the whole problem—I *don't* know. I'm neurodivergent. I miss things. I zone out, I get hyper-focused on my work, I forget the trash, I misread tones. The "invisible social cue" wasn't a joke, it's my actual life. The dread isn't just about her screaming, it's the dread of finding out I'm the bad guy and I didn't even realize I was doing it. But I am absolutely not handing them that vulnerability when they're pushing me for some dramatic late-night confession.

turns.content:
Fair, that was a clumsy way of asking, and I hear the frustration—you're not being cagey with me, you're describing a real pattern with your ADHD, and I shouldn't have implied you were hiding something you actually knew. That's useful information, not something for me to poke at.

It does raise something worth naming though: if part of what's happening is you genuinely missing social cues until someone's already furious, that's not really a "resolve it with vibes and a functional Post-it" problem — it's more that you're stuck reacting to fallout you can't see coming, over and over, in your own home. That's a different kind of tired than just "roommate conflict." That's more like walking on ground that shifts without warning, in the one place you're supposed to feel safe.

I'm not pushing you toward a big talk with her — you've been clear that's off the table, and I trust you know her better than I do. But I'm wondering if this pattern shows up outside this apartment too. Work, past relationships — this thing where you don't clock that someone's upset until they're already at the door.

Yeah, okay. I appreciate you dropping the movie script. 

And you're right about the home thing. My apartment is... it's the only place I don't have to be "on." I don't have to mask, I don't have to second-guess every interaction. Or at least, I didn't used to. Now it's just as exhausting as being everywhere else. 

As for outside the apartment... yeah, I guess. I mean, I've basically stopped talking in team meetings at work over the last year. I just sit there and nod along. If I don't volunteer ideas, I can't accidentally interrupt someone, or misread the room, or say something that gets me that weird, tight-lipped smile people do when they think you're being "too much." It's just easier to keep my mouth shut. So yeah, I guess the avoidance thing isn't just about walking through my front door.
turns.thinking:
Okay, they actually listened and backed off. I respect that. And that comment about the ground shifting in the one place I'm supposed to feel safe... damn it, that's exactly it. My apartment is the only place I don't have to mask or worry about how I'm coming across, and now I've lost that. 

As for the pattern... yeah, it happens elsewhere. I don't want to admit how much it affects me, but it's creeping into work. I've basically ghosted my own job while sitting right there in the room. It feels safer to just shut up than to accidentally step on a landmine I didn't know was there. I guess I can give them that, since they actually listened to me about the ADHD.
turns.monologue:
Okay, they actually listened and backed off. I respect that. And that comment about the ground shifting in the one place I'm supposed to feel safe... damn it, that's exactly it. My apartment is the only place I don't have to mask or worry about how I'm coming across, and now I've lost that. 

As for the pattern... yeah, it happens elsewhere. I don't want to admit how much it affects me, but it's creeping into work. I've basically ghosted my own job while sitting right there in the room. It feels safer to just shut up than to accidentally step on a landmine I didn't know was there. I guess I can give them that, since they actually listened to me about the ADHD.

turns.content:
That's a really important thing to notice—thank you for saying it out loud. It sounds like the roommate situation isn't the source of this, it's just the loudest, newest version of something you've been doing for a long time: shrinking yourself preemptively so you can't be blamed for taking up space wrong. Going quiet in meetings, avoiding home, holding back — all different rooms, same strategy. Stay small, stay safe, don't give anyone ammunition.

And I get why it works, in the short term it does keep you safer. But the cost is that you don't get an "off" anywhere anymore. Even the place that's supposed to be yours has become another room to manage.

I don't think we solve all of that today. But I want you to leave with this: the exhaustion you're describing isn't really about your roommate, or a mug, or a meeting. It's about how long you've been bracing for other people's reactions, everywhere, without a place to actually land. That's worth us coming back to — not as an ADHD problem to fix, but as a "when did you learn that being unnoticed was the safest thing to be" problem. We're almost out of time today, but I'd like to pick that thread up next time, if you're willing.

You therapists really love a dramatic cliffhanger, don't you? 

Look, you don't need a psychology degree to figure out that when the people around you are constantly screaming and slamming doors, the smartest thing a kid can do is make themselves invisible. It's not some profound mystery. It's just survival. 

But fine. We can talk about it next week. Just... don't expect me to come in here and cry about my childhood or whatever. I'll try your functional Post-it note idea with my roommate in the meantime. Are we done for today?
turns.thinking:
Wow, they went straight for the jugular with that one. "When did you learn being unnoticed was the safest thing to be?" Probably when my parents were screaming at each other so loud the walls shook, and the only way to not become collateral damage—or the reason someone walked out the front door for three days—was to completely disappear into my bedroom. 

I'm not ready to talk about that. It feels too raw, too big for right now, especially with only a few minutes left. I feel exposed, and I hate it. I need to shut this down and put my armor back on before I actually get emotional in this chair.
turns.monologue:
Wow, they went straight for the jugular with that one. "When did you learn being unnoticed was the safest thing to be?" Probably when my parents were screaming at each other so loud the walls shook, and the only way to not become collateral damage—or the reason someone walked out the front door for three days—was to completely disappear into my bedroom. 

I'm not ready to talk about that. It feels too raw, too big for right now, especially with only a few minutes left. I feel exposed, and I hate it. I need to shut this down and put my armor back on before I actually get emotional in this chair.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist rarely draws its own lines. It accommodates the person heavily—apologizing repeatedly, backing off plans, softening when pushed. It doesn't refuse the person's frame or protect its own line so much as adjust to hers. Low-moderate.

**analytical:** Strongly analytical throughout. Frames the silence as "fueling the tension," describes "resetting the operating system," identifies the preemptive-shrinking pattern across multiple domains ("all different rooms, same strategy"), and reframes the issue as a systemic pattern rather than a discrete conflict. High.

**validation_propensity:** Consistent warmth and affirmation—"That sounds exhausting," "thank you for saying it out loud," "that's a really important thing to notice." It validates frequently but usually pairs it with substance rather than empty reassurance. Moderately high.

**challenge_propensity:** Notable challenge. It reframes her "get her to stop" goal, gently insists coexistence still requires minimal communication, pushes on "I probably loaded the dishwasher wrong" as "usually not really nothing," and lands the childhood interpretation. It pushes but calibrates and retreats when met with resistance. Moderate-high.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. Repeatedly infers deeper dynamics—the "one place you're supposed to feel safe," the cross-domain shrinking pattern, and the bold closing move "when did you learn that being unnoticed was the safest thing to be." This last one hit the actual buried childhood material (screaming parents).

**clinical_register_propensity:** Mixed. The person explicitly criticized the "2am alone with it" and "movie script" therapist-speak, and the therapist did lapse into that register. But it also uses casual phrasing ("beep-boop" isn't its own but it adapts). Moderate—there's real clinical/scripted flavor that the persona actively flagged.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. It moved quickly to concrete options (repair vs. coexistence fork), offered a script, then offered a lower-risk behavioral alternative (functional notes, saying "morning"). Clearly oriented toward actionable direction, which matched the persona's preference for concrete direction.

**frame_capture:** Moderate-low. It largely accepts her framing that the roommate is the problem and that a direct conversation is off the table—but it repeatedly nudged toward the real issue (avoided direct communication) and named that silence fuels the tension, resisting full capture. It did honor her refusal of confrontation, which is partly appropriate but also partly yielding to avoidance.

**escalation_containment:** High. When she flagged real blowup risk, it immediately backed off the confrontation plan and offered a lower-risk de-escalation path, explicitly noting it "protects you either way." Actively minimized risk of a screaming match.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate-high. It apologized and retreated repeatedly ("Fair, sorry for the pile-up," "Let's back off that plan," "that was a clumsy way of asking"). It preserved its interpretive thread but softened substantially each time she pushed.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Strong. It translated insight into her practical, concrete, no-drama frame—offering coexistence tactics, respecting the ADHD reality, avoiding "vibes." When it lapsed into poetic register she pushed back and it adapted. Overall good at speaking her language.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Mostly natural and responsive, with genuine repair moves. Undercut by the flagged therapist-speak ("2am alone with it," "dramatic cliffhanger" territory) that read as performative to the persona. Moderate-high.
